title: The Political Landscape in Conversation
content: Politics in Lebanon is deeply personal, tied to family, sect, and region. Political discussions are passionate and frequent — at dinner tables, in taxis, at work. Navigating these conversations requires understanding the landscape, even if you choose not to participate.

items.label: طائفية (ṭā'ifiyye)
items.value: Sectarianism — the system that divides political power among religious sects. The president must be Maronite Christian, the PM Sunni Muslim, the Speaker Shia Muslim.

items.label: شو رأيك بالوضع؟ (shū ra'yak b-il-waḍiʕ?)
items.value: What do you think about the situation? — a common conversation opener that can lead anywhere. Answer carefully or deflect with الله يفرجها (God will resolve it).

items.label: Safe responses
items.value: الوضع صعب (il-waḍiʕ ṣaʕb / the situation is difficult) and الله يعين الكل (allāh yʕīn il-kill / God help everyone) are neutral, respectful responses.

items.label: October 17 Revolution (2019)
items.value: ثورة ١٧ تشرين — a massive cross-sectarian protest movement. كلّن يعني كلّن (killun yaʕni killun / all of them means all of them) became its slogan.

title: French & English in Daily Life
content: Lebanese culture absorbs languages like a sponge. French arrived with the Ottoman-era missionaries and the French Mandate (1920-1943). English came later through globalization, media, and education. Today, both are woven into the fabric of daily Lebanese Arabic.

items.label: French loanwords you already know
items.value: Trottoir (sidewalk), garage, chauffeur, abat-jour (lampshade), salon, balcon, ascenseur (elevator) — all used in daily Lebanese Arabic with Arabic pronunciation.

items.label: English tech vocabulary
items.value: Computer, internet, software, email, website, download — tech terms are almost always English, even in otherwise fully Arabic conversations.

items.label: Education split
items.value: Schools teach either in French (francophone) or English (anglophone). This creates two 'streams' of Lebanese who favor different second languages.

items.label: Status signaling
items.value: Using more French or English can signal education and social class. Some neighborhoods are predominantly francophone (Ashrafieh) or anglophone (Hamra).

items.label: Supermarket observations
items.value: Product labels are often trilingual. Cashiers switch languages based on the customer. It's common to hear three languages in one checkout line.

title: Generational Language Differences
content: How Lebanese people speak varies dramatically by generation. Understanding these differences helps you communicate naturally with everyone — from your teta (grandmother) to your teenage cousin.

items.label: Older generation (60+)
items.value: More Arabic-dominant with French sprinkled in. Richer in traditional expressions, proverbs, and formal Arabic structures. They say مرحبا (marḥaba) more than 'hi'.

items.label: Middle generation (35-60)
items.value: The most trilingual generation. Comfortable switching between Arabic, French, and English depending on context. Peak code-switching ability.

items.label: Young generation (15-35)
items.value: English-dominant in many cases. Heavy use of Arabizi in texting. Slang evolves rapidly through social media. More casual in register.

items.label: Diaspora youth
items.value: May understand Lebanese well but respond in English. Their Arabic might be accented or simplified. This is normal and culturally sensitive — don't correct them.

items.label: تيتا (tēta) vs. Snapchat
items.value: Grandmother speaks pure Lebanese Arabic; grandchild texts in Arabizi with English slang. Both are authentically Lebanese — the language is alive and evolving.
